segments.title: The 1805 invasion crisis and allied fleets
segments.summary: The strategic setting for the Trafalgar campaign: Spain's entry enlarged Napoleon's fleet, but the French and Spanish squadrons remained dispersed.
segments.body: Spain's December 1804 declaration of war increased the combined Franco-Spanish paper strength, while the quality and political reliability of senior French officers remained a concern. The decisive problem was geographical: fleets at Toulon, Cádiz, Brest, Rochefort, and other ports had to be brought together before they could cover an invasion of Britain.

evidence.note: The lecture sets out the 1805 fleet balance, Spanish entry, and dispersed French and Spanish squadrons.

segments.title: Napoleon's unworkable fleet timetables
segments.summary: Why the French strategy struggled: successive plans demanded precise, long-distance fleet movements while assuming weather and British reactions would cooperate.
segments.body: The lecture argues that Napoleon's plans required blockaded squadrons to escape on command, cross oceans quickly, and meet exact schedules. Frequent cancellations compounded the problem, leaving separate forces attempting to follow incompatible instructions and Villeneuve under extraordinary pressure.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke explains the repeated plans, their assumptions, and the confusion they produced.

segments.title: British mobilisation and sea-going experience
segments.summary: The Royal Navy could reinforce its fleets while keeping them at sea, turning mobilisation into practical experience and better-trained crews.
segments.body: British squadrons could retire toward the Channel Fleet, leave frigates to observe an enemy, and draw on ships and officers held in reserve. Time at sea was itself an advantage: crews and commanders gained practice while French forces remained constrained in port, and experienced rear admirals could even fill captaincies when needed.

evidence.note: The transcript contrasts British reinforcement and training at sea with the allied fleet's port constraints.

segments.title: Nelson's misdirection and Villeneuve's escape
segments.summary: Nelson tried to steer Villeneuve into a trap near the Spanish coast, but a merchant vessel exposed the deception and the French fleet slipped away.
segments.body: Nelson made his presence known near one route through the Strait of Gibraltar and expected Villeneuve to choose the other. A Spanish merchant brought word of the British position, however, while Villeneuve also lost the frigates meant to keep him under observation; the episode shows how fragile operational intelligence could be.

evidence.note: The lecture describes Nelson's ruse, the merchant warning, and Villeneuve's escape west of the Balearics.

segments.title: Cartagena, Cádiz, and the West Indies breakout
segments.summary: Villeneuve failed to collect ships at Cartagena, passed the British Strait squadron, and crossed the Atlantic after joining Gravina's Spanish force at Cádiz.
segments.body: At Cartagena, Spanish officers would not simply place their ships under Villeneuve without orders from their own government. After he passed the Strait, John Orde withdrew his ships of the line to reinforce the Channel Fleet while leaving frigates to report, and Villeneuve's Atlantic force was enlarged by Federico Gravina's squadron from Cádiz.

evidence.note: The transcript covers Cartagena's command dispute, Orde's response, and the Cádiz departure with Gravina.

segments.title: Martinique orders and a missed Caribbean opportunity
segments.summary: At Martinique, Villeneuve waited for Brest and hesitated to attack British possessions; news of Nelson then drove him back toward Europe.
segments.body: Villeneuve reached Fort-de-France expecting Ganteaume's arrival, but Brest remained blockaded and the combined force did little beyond the attack on Diamond Rock. Orders that eventually arrived envisaged a month of attacks on British colonies, yet reports that Nelson was nearby persuaded Villeneuve to abandon the Caribbean operation after only a short stay.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke describes the Martinique wait, delayed orders, Nelson reports, and the 11 June departure.

segments.title: Diamond Rock as a stone frigate
segments.summary: A small fortified rock off Martinique became a Royal Navy outpost whose capture cost the French and Spanish a disproportionately difficult operation.
segments.body: British sailors hauled guns up Diamond Rock and commissioned it as HMS Diamond Rock, using its position to watch the approaches to Fort-de-France. The garrison held the upper levels against a much larger force until water and ammunition ran short; its surrender followed a three-day attack, not a simple landing operation.

evidence.note: The lecture explains the fortification, armament, assault, siege conditions, and surrender of Diamond Rock.

segments.title: Nelson's Atlantic pursuit and British coverage
segments.summary: False reports sent Nelson south of the French fleet, yet British squadrons still covered the Mediterranean, Brest, and Ferrol approaches.
segments.body: Nelson reached Barbados soon after Villeneuve had left, but incorrect sightings kept him searching in the wrong direction before he returned across the Atlantic. The broader British response did not depend on one pursuit: Nelson covered the Mediterranean, Cornwallis guarded Brest, and Robert Calder was placed off Ferrol.

evidence.note: The transcript follows Nelson's delayed pursuit and explains the three British approaches to Europe.

segments.title: Cape Finisterre and Malta's two prizes
segments.summary: At Cape Finisterre in July 1805, Calder's outnumbered fleet fought a confused action in which HMS Malta captured two Spanish ships.
segments.body: The fleets sighted each other in the late morning of 22 July but did not engage until the afternoon, when the action became a scattered melee. HMS Malta was surrounded by five Spanish ships yet fought clear and captured the San Rafael and Firme, illustrating how individual ship performance could shape an indecisive fleet action.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke narrates the opening, duration, melee, and Malta's captures at Cape Finisterre.

segments.title: Why Calder did not renew the battle
segments.summary: Calder declined to attack again after Cape Finisterre, a choice shaped by damaged ships and the burden of escorting two captured prizes.
segments.body: The next day the fleets were separated, and Calder did not reopen the action despite having attacked while outnumbered the day before. The lecture points to the damaged Windsor Castle and Malta's two valuable prizes as practical constraints, while also distinguishing British operational standards from the less practiced allied fleet.

evidence.note: The transcript discusses the separation on 23 July, Calder's damaged ships and prizes, and fleet experience.

segments.title: Villeneuve withdraws to Cádiz
segments.summary: With the windward position available, the allied fleet still withdrew; Villeneuve's fears and the approach of Nelson and Collingwood set up the next stage of the campaign.
segments.body: On 24 July the Franco-Spanish fleet held the weather gauge but did not press an attack, a decision the lecture relates to its condition, experience, and the likelihood that Nelson would soon arrive. Villeneuve eventually entered Cádiz and abandoned a further attempt toward Brest after mistaking approaching ships for the Channel Fleet; Britain then sent Nelson and Collingwood south as the crisis deepened.

evidence.note: The closing section covers the weather-gauge decision, return to Cádiz, and the dispatch of Nelson and Collingwood.
